title: Waste tracking — where do we even start?

messages.content: We have zero waste tracking. The owner asked me to set something up but I've never done this before.

Start simple. You don't need software — you need a sheet of paper and a habit.

**Week 1 — Just observe and record:**

Put a clipboard in the kitchen with this format:

| Date | Time | Item | Quantity | Reason |
|------|------|------|----------|--------|
| | | | | |

**Reason categories** (keep it to 5):
- Expired
- Overproduction
- Prep error
- Customer return
- Damaged/dropped

Every time something goes in the bin, it goes on the sheet. Takes 10 seconds.

**Week 2 — Spot the pattern:**
At end of week 1, tally it up. You'll immediately see:
- What gets wasted most (usually milk, pastries, or prepped salads)
- When it happens (usually end of day = overproduction)
- The top reason category

**Week 3 — Act on the biggest one:**
If it's overproduction → adjust prep quantities based on sales data.
If it's expiry → review ordering frequency.
If it's prep errors → training issue.

Don't try to track everything perfectly from day one. Track anything at all — that's the breakthrough. Perfection kills waste tracking programs before they start.

This maps to Block B045 in your plan. Want me to mark it as in progress?
Yes, let's mark it. I put the clipboard up today.

Done — B045 Waste Tracking System is now marked as in progress.

One tip: take a photo of the clipboard at end of each day for the first week. If someone forgets to fill it in, you'll know by the gaps. And it gives you a backup in case the sheet gets coffee-stained or lost (it will).

Check back with me after a week and we'll review what the data shows.
